texts.orig: The aim of this review paper is to give an overview of the research activities of GKSS in the field of polymer based membranes. After summarizing the historic development of membrane science at GKSS, it describes different classes of polymeric materials for membranes, and the characterization of membranes. The design of membrane-based separation processes followed by examples of applications will also be presented. In the last chapter we will try to give an outlook for the future activities in membrane research.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation of materials is a fundamental issue in natural and technological processes. Separation by thermal means is well known, as the evaporation of salt-free water on the oceans or the distillation of single components from a mixed liquid. Distillation in technology is an energy-consuming process and in some cases also distillation cannot yield pure components easily, but only mixtures enriched in some components. Membrane based separation processes, on the other hand offer the possibility to separate mixtures at low energy consumption, i.e. under isothermal conditions. Life would not exist without membranes, since all types of cells are based on membranes separating their ingredients in a semi-permeable way from the surrounding media. Inside the cells a lot of cell organellae are also based on specific and even stimuliresponsive membranes to enable the necessary local concentrations of chemicals for the respective physiological purpose. A typical human body consists of 10 14 cells, each cell consists of typically 63000 l m 2 membrane area, which means a total of ca. 10 km 2 of membrane area per human body. Also the structure of organs can lead to membrane properties. An example is the kidney.

For the generation of drinking water from seawater, reverse osmosis using cellulose acetate became an important application of membranes, especially after World War II in Europe. Large scale desalination of water became possible after the invention of asymmetric membranes by Loeb and Sourirajan, [2] where the porosity of the membrane changes across its thickness. A thin 'dense' cover layer is supported mechanically by a rather porous thick layer, where the first is responsible for the selectivity and permeability, whilst the latter does not influence these properties significantly, thus leading to high fluxes at high selectivities.

texts.orig: Modern membrane science includes different aspects. The development of membranes happens in the chemical laboratory and - due to the improvement of computer power - also via molecular modelling. The membrane material itself must be located in a module and the design of modules depends on the process requirements as well as on the properties of the membrane. There are excellent reviews on membrane science available. [3,4] The aim of this review paper is to give an overview of the research activities of GKSS in the field of polymer based membranes. After summarizing the historic development of membrane science at GKSS, it describes different classes of polymeric materials for membranes, and the characterization of membranes. The design of membranebased separation processes followed by examples of applications will also be presented. In the last chapter we will try to give an outlook for the future activities in membrane research.

At this time the possibility of desalination by membranes using reverse osmosis was also discussed. First high-flux membranes from cellulose acetate were developed by K.W. Böddeker and co-workers in the Institute of Material Technology and Chemistry.

texts.orig: With the foundation of the Institute of Chemistry (first directors: H. Koske, 1983: H.D. Knauth) research was concentrated on membrane technology and environmental issues. Investigations into reverse osmosis (RO) and electrodialysis (ED) became less significant whilst research was intensified into applications of membranes for gas separation (GS) in under water and environmental technologies (purification and recovery of He of diving equipment, [9] enrichment of ethanol by pervaporation (PV) [10] and treatment of process solutions and waste water). The mathematical and thermodynamic basics and relevant modelling [11] supported the applicationoriented experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1987 R.D. Behling, an expert in gas and oil processes, took on the position as director of the institute. New organophilic membranes were developed for GS and PV, new or modified membrane materials based on silicon, polyetherimide, and multicompound systems, the manufacturing of thin film composite flat sheet and hollow fibre membranes have provided a diverse range of membranes for tests in pilot plants. In 1989 with the installation of a casting machine (1 m width), the reproducible manufacturing of flat sheet membranes became possible. Thus, sufficiently large quantities of membranes with sufficiently good qualities could be delivered for the separation of organic vapours from process and off-gases. New hybrid processes were designed, e.g. membrane separation combined with absorption and pressure swing adsorption. On an industrial scale, the first plant for recovery of gasoline vapour in tank farms was installed in 1989 and worked very successfully. This project was supported by industry and the Federal Ministry of Research and Technology.

texts.orig: The reunification of Germany brought new perspectives for the membrane research at GKSS: Recommended by the German Scientific Council, 1992 the Institute of Chemistry at Geesthacht was extended by a membrane group of the Institute of Polymer Chemistry at Teltow of the former Academy of Sciences of the GDR. Up to this time in Teltow, membrane research was focussed on the chemical and physical aspects of the transformation from polymer solution to morphology of solid membranes [12] and the experience was accumulated in the development of membranes for technical ultrafiltration (UF) [13] and RO, [14] polyelectrolyte membranes for PV [15] and hollow fibre membranes for blood purification. [16,17]

In 2005 the Institute was renamed into Institute of Polymer Research. Since then an additional focus of research has been directed at the design and synthesis of new (multi)functional polymers, with the aim of their application in regenerative medicine and engineering polymers, besides the ongoing membrane research.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Organic polymers are the dominating materials for gas separation membranes. Many polymers exhibit good gas selectivity and they can be easily processed into membranes. During the last two decades dozens of new polymers have been developed for gas separation. The largest group among these are polyimides. [19] In spite of these efforts less than 10 polymers are currently applied for industrial gas separations. Nearly all of these are technical polymers developed for totally different applications. 'Designer polymers' were either too expensive, their advantages over commercial polymers were not sensational enough or they did not show the expected performance under field conditions. The latter is especially true for modified (fluorinated) polyimides. Some of the 6FD-based polyimides showed outstanding separation properties in the laboratory but failed in real life due to plasticization or physical aging. Table 1 gives a list of polymers, which are of practical importance for gas separation.

texts.orig: GKSS selected polyetherimide because of its high helium/ nitrogen selectivity of 200 and its reasonably high He-permeability of 9.4 Barrer (1 Barrer = 10 -10 cm 3 cm/ cm 2 s cmHg). Preliminary calculations showed that the effective membrane thickness of PEI should be in the range of 0.1-0.2 l m. With thicker membranes the helium flux would not be high enough to compete with other purification methods. It was decided to develop an asymmetric membrane with a dense PEI top layer. Dichloromethane and 1,1,2-trichloroethane have been chosen as co-solvents and xylene and acetic acid as non-solvents. The solution was cast on a moving stainless steel belt and the membrane was precipitated in acetone after a few seconds evaporation time. [9] Asymmetric membranes with pore-free top layers thinner than 0.2 l mand helium/nitrogen-selectivities of up to 240 could be obtained using a continuously operating membrane casting machine. It is noteworthy that a number of membranes obtained by this procedure showed a substantially higher selectivity than the intrinsic one of polyetherimide. Usually the selectivity of polymeric gas separation membranes is independent of film thickness. However, for very thin films of glassy polymers intrinsic polymer properties like densities might change with film thickness. An increased density and gas selectivity has been observed with decreasing film thickness. [20] The PEImembrane preparation process described above was transferred to a pilot production machine and a helium purification plant with a capacity of 100 m 3 /h was built. [21] For the preparation of asymmetric membranes with thin dense top layers a solvent with a low boiling point is beneficial. For PEI only halogenated solvents with high volatilities were known. texts.label: text
texts.orig: An important material class for gas separation membranes are organic/inorganic hybrid materials. The inorganic compound can be an impermeable filler leading to an increased compaction resistance of the membrane under high pressure conditions, but surprisingly, impermeable fillers can also increase selectivities of gas separation membranes. Inorganic fillers can be permeable and selective like zeolites or carbon molecular sieves and contribute to the membrane selectivity, or inorganic fillers can react reversibly with one of the components to be separated (carrier membrane). These four classes of organic/inorganic hybrid materials for gas separation will be introduced briefly.

High pressures like 80 bar for natural gas treatment are no problem for the dense selective layer, but the microporous support layer might compact under the pressure applied leading to a reduced membrane permeability. A 60 % flux reduction has been observed for a microporous PEI membrane after 100 hrs exposure to natural gas under 80 bar. texts.label: text
texts.orig: For many relevant industrial applications of gas separation membranes swelling of the polymeric material is a problem. An example is the treatment of natural gas. One potentially large membrane application is hydrocarbon dewpointing, which is basically a butane/methane separation. Some polymers have huge ideal selectivities for this separation, when tested with pure gases, e.g. a selectivity of 210 for polyhexadecylsiloxane. But when tested with real gas mixtures these selectivities may drop to values below 3. [23] This selectivity decrease is caused by the high solubility of one gas component, in this case butane, leading to excessive swelling and increased permeability of the slow gas. One method to reduce this problem is the confinement of the polymer into the pores of the substrate membrane. Due to this confinement swelling is restricted and the selectivity decrease is less drastic. [23] Another method is the in-situ formation of an inorganic network in the polymer matrix. Composite membranes were obtained from silicone/silica hybrids, prepared by a sol-gel process. The introduction of silica nanoparticles could double the membrane selectivity for butane/methane separation. [24]

texts.orig: Nanocomposite membranes based on glassy polymers with high free volume have only been prepared by means of the addition of non-porous fillers like fumed silica to the polymer matrix. [25-27] For some of these nanocomposite membranes a significant increase in permeability has been reported, resulting from the ability of these fillers to disrupt polymer chain packing and to increase the system free volume. The effect on permeability and selectivity of these nanocomposite membranes can be understood by taking into account the transport mechanism for the high free volume polymers, which occurs by competitive sorption and surface diffusion. For some systems a simultaneous increase of permeability and selectivity has been reported. [25] Recently, we synthesized nanocomposite membranes based on poly(1-trimethylsilyl-1propyne) (PTMSP) and silica by sol-gel copolymerisation of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) with different organoalkoxysilanes in tetrahydrofuran solutions of PTMSP. The influence of the synthesis parameters on the silica conversion and the gas permeation performance of PTMSP-silica nanocomposite membranes were investigated. Both the butane permeability and butane/methane selectivity could be increased by the incorporation of silica by a sol-gel process. [28]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the most attractive approaches to overcome the selectivity limitations of polymeric membranes has been the addition of microporous molecular sieving materials like zeolites or carbon molecular sieves. Inorganic molecular sieves exhibit a much higher selectivity for many gas mixtures than polymeric membranes due to their very defined pore structure. However, the preparation of defect-free zeolite layers on a large scale is extremely difficult and it seems doubtful that this will ever be achieved at a competitive price. Hence, the combination of the superior gas selectivities of molecular sieves with the simple processibility of polymers have attracted many researchers. Hybrid membranes consisting of inorganic molecular sieves and polymers are often referred to as mixed matrix membranes. [29] It was believed for a long time that the incorporation of a molecular sieve in a polymer matrix does not change the polymer selectivity under steady sate conditions. [30] Kulprathipanja from UOP showed for the first time, that the addition of the hydrophobic zeolite silicalite to cellulose acetate changed the membrane's selectivity drastically. [31] It was shown later by Jia et al. from GKSS that sub-micrometer sized zeolite crystals can be incorporated into silicone rubber and manufactured into composite membranes. [32,33] It was shown that the incorporation of the zeolite crystals facilitated the transport of smaller molecules and hindered transport of larger molecules.

texts.orig: Carrier facilitated transport involves a combination of chemical reaction and diffusion. The membrane phase contains a carrier agent that is chemically combined with the permeant to be transported. The carrier might be mobile or immobile in the membrane material. In the first case the carrier molecule reacts with one component of the gas mixture on the upstream side of the membrane. The carrier complex diffuses through the membrane and decomposes on the downstream side and the permeant is released. The carrier then diffuses back to the feed side to react again with a new gas molecule. In case of immobile carriers the permeating compound moves from one carrier site to the next one in a hopping mechanism, sometimes this transport mechanism is described as bucket brigade. Carrier facilitated transport has been proposed and tested for the separation of gas mixtures which are difficult to separate with passive membranes. One of the most important processes in the petrochemical industry is the separation of gaseous olefin/paraffin mixtures. Low temperature distillation is the conventional but energy and cost intensive way to perform the separation. Facilitated transport membranes could be an attractive alternative. Silver ions are well known to form reversible complexes with olefins. Thus silver salts are well suitable as carrier agent. [34] About 15 years ago the application of silver salt/polymer blends with very high silver loadings as membrane material was proposed for selective olefin transport. [35] It was shown that the silver salt acted as effective barrier for the paraffin transport but facilitated the olefin transport. The propene/propane selectivity obtained with a 10 vol% propene mixture ranged from 110 to 250 depending on the permeate pressure. The problem is the long term stability of this membrane type due to silver ion reduction to metallic silver. Some optimisation steps leading to more stable membranes have been published some years ago. [36]

texts.orig: The most commonly used polymers for the preparation of ultrafiltration membranes are polysulfone, polyethersulfone, polyvinylidenefluoride, cellulose and polyacrylonitrile. Nearly all commercial membranes are asymmetric membranes prepared by the so-called phase inversion process. For this process the polymer is dissolved in a water miscible solvent like dimethylacetamide, dimethylformamide or N-methylpyrolidone. The concentration range is 10 to 20wt%. The viscous solution is cast on a support, e.g. a polyester non-woven and then immersed in a water bath. Water diffuses in the polymer/solvent film and solvent diffuses into the water. The pore structure is generated by phase separation. A large variety of pore sizes and pore distributions can be generated by this simple process. [37] Only a few UF composite membranes are available. At GKSS low molecular weight cut-off composite membranes from cellulose and a polyetherblock -polyamide were developed. Cellulose UF membranes are used in applications where low fouling characteristics are required. Cellulose has a very regular structure and is able to form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the several hydroxy groups. As a result cellulose is practically insoluble in almost all organic solvents. Today most of the cellulose membranes are prepared by hydrolysis of asymmetric cellulose acetate membranes in strong alkali solutions. [38] An alternative for the preparation of cellulose composite membranes with a low molecular weight cut-off was developed. [39] Cellulose was reacted with hexamethyldisilazane. Trimethylcellulose (TMSC) with a degree of substitution 2.61 was obtained. Solutions of TMSC in cyclohexane were used to coat microporous PAN- membranes. The cellulose was then regenerated by treatment with dilute hydrochloric acid. The resulting cellulose composite membranes had water fluxes of 20 l/m 2 h bar and cut-offs below 3000 g/ mol. This competes very well with commercial cellulose membranes.

texts.orig: The most common materials for reverse osmosis membranes are cellulose acetate, polyamide and the 'thin-film composites' prepared by interfacial polymerisation on the surface of a porous support. The majority of today's commercial NF and RO membranes are prepared by the interfacial polymerisation method. A review of these composite membranes was published by Petersen. [41] One of the most successful TFC membranes is the FT-30 developed by Cadotte [42] in the North Star Laboratories and presently commercialised by General Electric. The reaction involved in the preparation of the FT-30 is shown in Figure 5.

texts.orig: As it is very hard to bring completely new and specially designed polymers into the membrane market, the need to understand polymer structure/properties relationship of polymers for gas separation became the object of systematic studies. [45] Together with improving computer supported modelling capabilities, the targeted development of new polymers with improved properties seemed to be at the edge. A systematic work to understand structure/properties relationships was started at GKSS. The polymer system selected at first was based on maleimide vinylether copolymers. [46] The monomers copolymerise via a charge transfer mechanism and result in strictly alternating copolymers as seen in Figure 7.

texts.orig: Aromatic polymers seemed to be a better choice for tailoring free volume. In the early 1990 a very comprehensive overview on polymer gas permselectivity appeared. [49] By plotting permeability over selectivity of all available polymers an upper bound was empirically detected showing the state of the art of polymers for gas separation. Polyimides (PIs) were found close to the upper bound. Polyimides with highest gas permeabilities were manufactured by DuPont and were reported by Hayes. [50] The principle to construct polymers with high gas permeability was detected earlier by Hoehn and Richter. [51] The polymers should contain: (a) at least one rigid divalent subunit with no colinear bond extensions (b) at least one bond should be unable to rotate around 360 °C for sterical reasons (c) 50 % or more of the main chain atoms are aromatic. It turned out that poly(amide imide)s (PAI) were more or less not investigated with respect to gas permselectivity. In addition, PAIs are also found to be very suitable hosts for generating polymer/metal nanocomposites useful in membrane reactor applications. [52-54] A rich variation in structures of pure PAIs is apparent from Figure 8.

Application of molecular modelling to the polymer with highest gas permeability found so far (amorphous poly(1-(trimethylsilyl)-1-propyne) (PTMSP)) the calculated packing density of 1.22 g/cm 3 was considerable higher than the experimental results of 0.7-0.8 g/cm 3 . texts.orig: Dendrimeric polymers have a high number of end groups due to the successive reaction of AB2 monomers including protecting and deprotecting steps and allow to functionalise these groups for, e.g. catalyst binding. Besides having the lack of the perfect symmetry of dendrimers also hyperbranched polymers can come close to a high concentration of end groups within one macromolecule. The advantage of hyperbranched polymers is the lower cost of synthesis, because those polymers are usually prepared in one step using specially prepared AB2 monomers to avoid cross-linking and gelation. [69,70] The A2 + B3 approach is less frequently applied and mostly results in cross-linked or gelated polymers. Usual techniques to avoid gelation are stopping the polymerisation at low conversions by either precipitation or endcapping. This approach was followed to produce hyperbranched poly(ether sulfone)s and poly(ether ketone)s by polycondensation of either free or silylated trihydroxyB3 monomers (see Fig. 11). [71-73]

texts.orig: Even at complete conversion no cross-linking occurs under the conditions of exact 1/1 stoichiometry for non-silylated B3 -monomers (with the exception of phloroglucinol -only a mixture of linear oligomers was obtained [73] ). Silylated monomers even tolerated a 10 mol% excess of the A2 -monomer. End group functionalisation of the silylated or free hydroxyl groups was possible, however, only a part of the theoretically available end groups could be reacted. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF) revealed the formation of significant fractions of cyclic oligomers and polymers thus explaining the diminution of the free pendant hydroxy groups. This effect was found more pronounced with the smallest B3 monomer, the phloroglucinol. [73] With silylated phloroglucinol up to a 30mol% excess of the A2 component could be added without cross-linking at complete conversion. However, multicyclic, internally bridged polymers resulted with only a minor amount of free, reactive end groups. Most probably due to sterical restrictions the bulkier, larger B3 -monomers (Fig. 10) produced a higher number of free, reactive end groups.

texts.orig: polymers is sophisticated because two bonds have to be combined together simultaneously for chain growing. The method of using silylated monomers for polymerisation was invented about 20 years ago. [77] The synthetic route described in [78] generally allows cleaner polycondensation conditions as no water as side product is evolved and the generated silylether side products are less reactive and highly volatile. Applying the silyl-method to polymerize this type of ladder polymers with intrinsic microporosity lead to unexpected results: [79] (i) the reaction is highly exothermic and the polymerisation has to be started at room temperature for a while with stepwise temperature increase for complete conversion. (ii) A strong solvent effect was detected. (iii) By MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry cyclic products were detected as e.g. C3 and C4. As solvents N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), and sulfolane were selected. NMP yielded higher inherent viscosities than DMSO. DMSO and sulfolane were about similar. The cyclic products detected by MALDITOF were similar for NMP and DMSO showing both, even and to a much lower content also odd cycles. However, using sulfolane as solvent lead besides exclusively even cycles also to linear chains with exclusively spirobisindane hydroxy end groups. At higher molar masses to the end of the detection limit the signals showed about similar intensity for cycles and linear chains, at low masses the signal intensity for cycles dominated. By choice of solvent the isolated cycles could be purified. The insoluble residue from dimethylacetamide treatment showed by MALDI-TOF exclusively signals of even cycles up to molar masses above 10000 g/mol. Computer simulation using the HYPERCHEM ® software [79] demonstrated that the contorted structure at the spiroatom of the spirobisindane unit might be bended and can be regarded as more flexible than expected. The different fine structure of the synthesised polymer by using NMP/DMSO or sulfolane as solvent cannot be explained by dipole moments or dielectric constant. However, the donicity of NMP/DMSO is much higher compared to sulfolane and the chain growing mechanism seems to be influenced in favour to even cycles and linear chains.

texts.orig: By IUPAC definition membrane reactors are a ' device for simultaneously carrying out a reaction and membrane-based sepa- ration in the same physical enclosure '. [80] This definition may be extended to 'membranes containing a catalyst' because they can be used as membrane contactor without any separation and may still show improvement over other reactors. Comprehensive reviews found for membrane reactors are based more on the engineering view [81] or the function of membrane and catalyst in the membrane reactor. [82-84] In almost all membrane reactor processes the reaction is supported by a catalyst and the environment of the catalyst can markedly influence its activity and therefore the performance of the total process. [84] Inorganic membranes such as alumina or silica consist of materials widely used as catalyst supports and together with metal membranes of catalytic materials (mainly Pd) have been investigated since about 1965. [85] Their main advantage is the high temperature stability up to 1000 °C. Biocatalysts, on the other hand, do not require or are even decomposed at high temperatures and more versatile polymeric supports have been the first choice. [86] At GKSS it was decided to focus on polymers as materials for membrane reactors with emphasis on combined catalyst/membrane assembly. In biocatalysis, there are numerous other reactions running at temperatures below 200 °C and improved catalysts or membrane reactor processes may run at lower temperatures compared to state of the art processes to improve the catalysts lifetime and save energy. Thus, the lower temperature stability of polymers may be balanced. We focused our research on catalyst containing membranes as the essential part of the membrane reactor process. Both, porous and nonporous, catalytically reactive membranes were developed.

texts.orig: With the development of poly(amide imide) polymers [52,55-57] materials were available to host nano-sized metal catalysts [52,53] by a simple preparation method. The polymer was solved in an appropriate solvent to about 10 % solution. Serving as catalyst precursor, a metal salt dissolved in the same solvent was added, stirred for at least 8 h and cast as a film. After complete, slow evaporation of the solvent a clear, transparent film was formed containing the metal salt evenly distributed in the polymeric matrix. A chemical reduction by 1% NaBH4/methanol solution or the thermal decomposition of the catalyst precursor yielded well distributed nanosized catalyst clusters throughout the film. By this way a metal concentration up to 30 wt-% could be generated. A detailed analysis of these polymer/metal nanocomposites by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray absorption fine-structure spectroscopy (XAFS), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) revealed their homogeneous distribution and in some cases a bimodal distribution. [54,87] The metal cluster sizes ranged from 1 to about 7 nm depending on the host polymer, the metal, and the preparation conditions. Long mixing times of > 8 h of salt and polymer improved the homogeneity of the cluster sizes. As detected by Extended XAFS (EXAFS), the Pd-clusters are covered by a single oxide layer. At room temperature the high reactivity of the Pd-clusters were demonstrated by reaction to H2 and O2. Without any pre-treatment the oxide shell was removed and H2 adsorbed by the Pd as detected by a drastic increase in time-lag for H2 [53] The activity as hydrogenation catalyst of the Pd was tested by nitrous oxide decomposition showing that with at least stoichiometric amounts of H2 no nitrous oxide permeated unreacted through the catalytic membrane. [53] In heterogeneous reactions, the available surface of the catalyst mainly determines the performance. Calculating the active metal surface inside a 1 and 5 l m thick membrane film of 1 m 2 , 15% loading and various cluster sizes demonstrated the strong dependence of the surface on the cluster size. At about 2 nm cluster size for the 5 l mfilm a surface area of the catalyst of about 300 m 2 is available per m 2 of membrane. The use as hydrogenation catalyst of polymer/metal nanocomposites was further tested by propene hydrogenation, [88] chlorophenol hydrodehalogenation coupled to pervaporation, [89,90] and hydrodehalogenation of chloroorganic compounds in ground water. [91]

texts.orig: Unsupported Pd-clusters can be prepared inside PDMS using the method described above to about 5 wt-%, but the preparation of thin-film composite membranes on large scale would be laborious. Therefore nanoscale Pd-catalysts on titania and silica supports were prepared and the supported catalysts were added to PDMS solutions to give catalytically reactive composite membranes. [91] Large membrane areas can be produced easily by this method. The activity of the thin-film composite membranes was within the range of commercial alumina supported catalysts. The membranes were used as pictured in Figure 14.

texts.orig: Chlorobenzene dissolved in water (0.5 g/10 L water) was fed alongside the membrane and recirculated. The hydrophobic chlorobenzene immediately sorbed inside the PDMS membrane. H2 permeated from the gas phase at the back side of the membrane to the catalytic membrane layer, activated the Pd-catalyst and dehydrohalogenated the sorbed chlorobenzene to benzene and hydrochloric acid. Benzene and HCl diffused to the outer membrane phase and dissolved in the water. The reaction progress could be followed by pH-decrease. Some catalyst deactivation was observed after prolonged reaction times. An improved catalyst, e.g. based on Pt, is expected to be more stable for long-time runs at room temperature.

texts.orig: Porous catalytic membranes were developed for hydrogenation, dimerization and enzymatic conversion. Nitrate decomposition of ground water by hydrogenation was studied by ultrafiltration hollow-fibre membranes (HF) containing supported Pd/Cu-catalyst. [92] The H2 was inserted without any gas bubbles by a HF-module to saturate nitrate containing water by the reducing agent. This solution was fed through the catalytic membrane. The function of pH, through-flow and temperature was studied. By regulation of the through-flow the contact time to the catalyst could be tuned and as a result the toxic side product ammonia was minimized.

texts.orig: Dimerization of lower alkenes such as isobutene to isooctene is in use to produce isooctane as fuel additive. The reaction is acid catalysed and yields mixtures of dimers, trimers and oligomers by a cationic mechanism. We developed porous membranes with strong acid catalysts inside the pores, mainly silica supported Nafion ® (Nafion ® SAC, DuPont). [94] By choice of the membrane polymer (as binder) reactive membranes with various pore sizes and properties were created. Isobutene was fed in a forced-flow mode passing the membrane. At conversions above about 10% the reaction products (dimers, trimers mainly) condensate inside the pores and were collected at the reactor outlet in liquid form. Depending mainly on the binder either a high conversion (98 % conversion at 22 % selectivity to isooctene; Binder: Hyflon ® AD) or high selectivity (45 % conversion at > 80 % selectivity to isooctene; Binder: Teflon ® AF) resulted. The space velocity of the process was suited for technical applications and no catalyst poisoning was detected within run times of one week.

texts.orig: Porous membranes for enzymatic reactions for polysaccharide syntheses [95] were produced from commercial capillary pore membranes with diameters of 0.4, 1.0, and 3.0 l m. The enzyme (fructosyltransferase) was covalently bound to the surface by a previously photo-grafted layer of poly(2-aminoethyl methacrylate) to the membrane surface. The flow through the membrane was not significantly altered by this layer. In the flow-through mode glucose was converted to inulin at about 80 % with a molecular weight of about 30 Mio. Dalton. Deactivation of the enzyme was observed after 12 h of operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The application of polymeric membranes in the field of energy generation gained significance in the last decade with the growing interest in fuel cells as an alternative energy converter for automobiles and portable devices. In the fuel cell technology the membrane has the crucial function of promoting the proton transport from the anode to the cathode and simultaneously acts as a barrier between the main reactants: the fuel (usually hydrogen or methanol) and oxygen. The main requirements for such membrane are high proton conductivity and chemical stability. Although the development of membranes for fuel cell is worldwide under investigation in many laboratories, Nafion ® , the ionomeric membrane produced by DuPont still dominates the market. Nafion ® has a fluorinated hydrophobic backbone and side chains terminated with sulfonic groups. Among the few alternatives are Flemion ® (Asahi Glass) and Aciplex ® (Asahi Chemical) with structures similar to Nafion ® . The great challenge for membrane researchers is to develop new materials able to overcome the known Nafion ® drawbacks: (i) reduction of the proton conductivity at temperatures above 100 °C (relevant for the application in hydrogen fuel cells for the automotive industry) and (ii) excessive methanol and water permeability (relevant for the application in direct methanol fuel cell).

texts.text: Application in Direct Methanol Fuel Cells (DMFC)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes for DMFC have been an important research topic at GKSS aiming the development of fuel cells for portable application. [104-114] The most favoured approach in this case has been the use of organic-inorganic materials. Different sulfonated polyether ketones have been used as polymer matrix with addition of zirconium oxide and different forms of silica or silicates. A comparison between inorganic fillers with different aspect ratios (fumed silica, layered silicates and tubular silicates) has been recently published. [109] In the same paper, the influence of the surface modification has been confirmed as much more relevant than that of the aspect ratio. Besides the surface modification with basic groups aiming at the compatibilisation with the polymer matrix, the covalent bonding of proton conductive components such as heteropolyacids to fumed silica has been reported as a strategy to inhibit the solubility and consequently bleeding out of the heteropolyacids. [108] Zirconium phosphate has been used by different groups as additive for fuel cell membranes. [97,98] The development of composites based on zirconium phosphate has been early explored at GKSS. The treatment with basic polymers such as polybenzimidazole has been used to improve the adhesion to the polymer matrix and promote the exfoliation, leading also to an improved performance in the direct methanol fuel cell. [115] The organic-inorganic composites have been investigated with the help of advanced characterization methods such as anomalous small angle X-ray scattering (ASAXS) at the synchrotron facilities at DESY/ HASYLAB. ASAXS allows the independent analysis of the distribution of inorganic phases containing different elements.

texts.orig: Block copolymers are a fascinating class of polymeric materials, since they offer the possibility to combine different properties along one macromolecular chain. The morphological features of block copolymers, especially tailor-made diblock, triblock and higher block copolymers of different chain topologies have been an increasingly intense research subject during the last decades. Block copolymers composed of chemically different chain sequences tend to segregate on an intramolecular level, if there are repulsive interactions between dissimilar blocks. Due to the connectivity of the blocks a macroscopic phase separation is suppressed and instead a so-called microphase separation on the length scale of the macromolecular chains occurs, which leads to a self-assembly into different microphase morphologies. These depend on the chemical composition, topology of the macromolecule, degree of polymerization [116] and also often on the way of preparation of the final bulk [117,118] or thin film material. [119] Some examples for morphologies, which could be interesting for membranes are shown in Figure 16. The morphologies found for diblock copolymers, other well-defined linear or branched block copolymers with more components [120,121] or their blends [122-125] show even more complex microphase morphologies.

texts.orig: While well-defined diand triblock copolymers have found wide applications in the field of thermoplastic elastomers (like polystyreneblock -polybutadieneblock -polystyrene) or as viscosity modifiers in water-based systems (like poly(ethylene oxide)block -poly(propylene oxide)block -poly(ethylene oxide)), much less studies on their use as membranes exist. By using a selective sorbent pore diameters in a polystyreneblock -poly(methyl methacrylate) diblock copolymer could be tuned in a range from 200 to 500 l m. These microporous membranes showed a tremendous flux for gases but retained still some selectivity for some gas pairs, which can be explained by Knudsen diffusion. [126] Also selective swelling of blocks during the membrane formation is a potential strategy for the formation of block copolymer-based membranes with tuned pore sizes. Ikkala et al. have demonstrated for blends of polystyreneblock -poly(4-vinylpyridine) diblock copolymers with low molecular weight amphiphiles that the guest amphiphile can be washed out in a subsequent step leading to materials with controlled pore sizes. [127,128] Similar experiments were carried out by Russell's group with blends of a diblock copolymer and a homopolymer, where the homopolymer could be removed selectively after film formation and lead to channel sizes of controllable diameters. [129,130]

texts.orig: Block copolymers composed of styrene and butadiene have been studied in terms of their gas permeability. [132] These authors also studied the influence of the membrane preparation, i.e. they compared solution casting with compression moulding. Another block copolymer studied was based on styrene and vinyltrimethylsilane. [133] Besides the gas separation, also the permeability for ions and other watersoluble molecules like amino acids and proteins have been investigated. The ion permeability was studied for triblock copolymers composed of hydrophobic polystyrene end blocks and a hydrophilic poly( c -benzyl glutamate) centre block. [134] Modifications after polymerization increase the possibilities to influence the permeability and selectivity of the final membrane. For example, the centre block of a polystyreneblock -polyisopreneblock -polystyrene triblock copolymer was used for a radiation induced grafting polymerization of 4-vinylpyridine and 1-vinylimidazole. These grafted block copolymers were modified with Co-containing Schiff bases to enhance their permeability for oxygen. [135-137] Polyisopreneblock -polystyreneblock -polyisopreneblock -poly(4-vinylbenzyl dimethylamine)block -polyisoprene pentablock terpolymers forming a three layered lamellar structure, which was conserved after sequential quaternization of the amine block, crosslinking of the isoprene blocks and sulfonation of the styrene block. The resulting charge mosaic membranes exhibited good cation and anion exchange capability, thus leading to reverse osmosis. [138,139] These membranes also showed pHdependent permeability for amino acids. [140] Later similar properties were reported for polystyreneblock -polybutadieneblock -poly(4-vinylpyridine) triblock terpolymer with lamellar structure, which was chemically treated in a similar way. [141,142]

texts.orig: A general problem in the preparation of block copolymer based membranes is the orientation of their microdomains on a macroscopic scale. For example, cylindrical channels should cross a membrane and should not be oriented in parallel to the plane of the membrane. Casting block copolymers as thin separating layers on a support requires therefore a certain directing force. As mentioned before, Russell's group investigated the influence of the evaporation of a solvent, which leads to oriented channels in diblock copolymers [154] and blends of diblock copolymers with a homopolymer. [129,130] An external field like an electric DC-field can also guide the selfassembly of block copolymer melts or solutions, as was shown for cylindrical and lamellar block copolymers. [155,156] Using such strategies might be the way to align cylinders or lamellae of a block copolymer into the right direction. While so far only studies were reported, where block copolymer films were generated on top of a homogeneous substrate (like a Si wafer), real membranes have to be prepared on a porous substrate enabling a flux of permeate across the separating block copolymer layer. First experiments in this direction have been carried out, but will require more efforts in the future. [157]

texts.text: 3.2.1. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and its Preparation Methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mass transport mechanism of membranes depends on chemical and physical interactions between membrane and the mixture to be separated. The morphology, i.e. pore size and distribution, material composition, layer thickness and tortuosity can be investigated and visualized directly by microscopic methods. Due to its high resolution, large depth of field and variety of different contrast mechanisms, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) is the commonly used instrument for microscopic membrane investigation. Viewing perpendicularly onto the separation layer of membrane samples, pore-structure, -size and -distribution are visible in the secondary electron (SE) contrast. For membranes manufactured with material composites like inorganic fillers or catalytic particles, the material contrast of backscattered electrons (BSE) shows the material composition, particle size and distribution (Fig. 15). Thickness and morphology of different membrane layers can be determined by investigation of a cross section. The quality of all microscopic methods depends on the quality of the sample preparation. Drying, sectioning, staining and conductive coating are the preparation methods mainly employed for SEM investigation. All these methods bear the risk of getting preparation artefacts.

texts.orig: A new application of SFM, called pneumatic scanning force microscopy (PSFM), which has been developed especially for porous materials, is coming up. [161] The purpose of PSFM is to obtain more specific information about the properties of individual pores by observing the influence of the pores on a gas streaming through them. In order to compare the image quality of SFM and PSFM, pyramid-shaped siliconnitride tips of lower height and a V-shaped cantilever were also used. Common SFM and PSFM image of a single sample are directly comparable.

texts.orig: Exact knowledge of material properties and parameters of separation performance is necessary for the optimal use of all kinds of membranes. An essential investigation of the performance and quality of gas separation membranes is the measurement of their permeability and selectivity. Due to the lack of suitable commercial equipment to perform these tests, GKSS designed a test facility for the determination of gas permeation properties of membranes for single gases or binary mixtures and multicomponent systems.

On the other side, the penetration depends on the geometry and the kinetic diameter of the gas molecule and its way through the polymeric material. Therefore one can use the dynamics of a permeation experiment, i.e. the time-lag, before the permeability coefficient P reaches its steady-state, to determine the diffusion coefficient. [162] For the determination of diffusion coefficients of liquids in polymer matrices, isotopic tracer techniques were the experimental mainstay [163] in the past. Today, pulsed field gradient NMR (PFG-NMR) spin-echo techniques are often the method of choice for obtaining the required data. [164] For studying the transport properties in membranes this method is used by GKSS for the determination of diffusion coefficients of solvents in polymer matrices, e.g. solvents in polyetherblock -polyamide (PEBAX). texts.orig: A pressure increase test unit is suitable to characterize the permeation behaviour of gases and vapours in membranes. The basic principle is a simple pressure measurement, which is independent on the type of gas. A computer automatically controls the whole measurement, including the filling, refilling and evacuation process. Only the membrane has to be changed manually. The dependence of permeability from temperature and swelling can be measured in one step. As an example, Figure 21 shows the permeation behaviour of n-butane in PDMS. One important design feature is the quality control of each measurement by an automatic registration of the measuring steps by means of a data report. The unit of permeability is defined as the volumetric flowrate (mN 3 /h), which penetrates a square meter membrane area by one bar

texts.orig: If one is switching over from gas separation to the separation of liquids or liquid/solid systems, the processes are nano-, ultra-, or microfiltration. While gas separation is based on the interaction of the gas molecules with the membrane material under the influence of solubility and diffusion of a molecule through the material, the filtration processes are simple sieving mechanisms. The fundamental assumption is that molecules with a defined hydrodynamic radius [166] are unable to pass through the pores of a microporous membrane while others of smaller size will penetrate. On closer examination one will see that interactions of the molecule with the solvent or with the membrane surface or both will falsify the investigation. [167]

texts.orig: During a Bubble point measurement (also gas-liquid displacement porosimetry, GLDP) the membrane or filter is filled with a wetting liquid and a gas pressure is applied and increased. This fluid displacement techniques are specially appropriate as they are non-destructive, very fast and refer only to pores opened to flux, which are the only pores which really contribute to membrane permeation. These advantages were recognised from the pioneering works of Erbe, later improved by Grabar and Nikitine. [167a] The differential pressure which is necessary for the gas bubble to displace the liquid from the pore is used to calculate that pore's diameter by the Young-Laplace equation assuming a circular cylindrical pore geometry and complete wetting.

texts.orig: For this method pore filling liquids with low surface tension and low vapour pressure are required. Bubbles (representing gas penetration) can be determined visually or by electronic flow sensors. As the pressure is increased the liquid is removed from pores with smaller diameters. By measuring the total volume of gas (the displacing fluid), as a function of differential pressure, a pore size distribution can be calculated. Unfortunately, this method is subject to many measurement uncertainties and will often predict a too low size of the largest pore. For example, different results are obtained for different liquids. In addition, the rate of pressure increase and the pore length may influence the result. Improved equipment designs and the use of empirical correction factors can decrease the measurement uncertainty. The method is typically used for materials with pore diameter > 0.15 l m due to the high transmembrane pressure (TMP) which is required for displacing the liquid from smaller pores. [22a,167b]

texts.orig: In order to apply membrane technology to a specific separation problem, be it a chemical or petrochemical production process, an effluent treatment facility or a medical device, to name only a few, it is necessary to integrate the membrane material into an apparatus. Such an apparatus is called a membrane module. Its basic, external design features are a feed connection, a retentate connection and a permeate connection. For some pervaporation, gas and vapour permeation processes, an additional sweep gas supply is employed on the permeate side. Figure 23 shows a principal sketch of the arrangement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Depending on the geometrical form of the membrane material, i.e. flat sheet or tubular, a multitude of membrane modules was designed. The most popular versions are probably the hollow fibre module for tubular membranes and the spiral wound module for flat sheet membranes. However, other types as tubular or capillary for tubular membrane configurations and plate-and-frame types, envelope types or rotating disk types for flat sheet membranes have been suggested and introduced into industrial practice. Melin and Rautenbach [3] as well as the information material of different membrane process equipment suppliers give information on the different designs. An advantageous design for flat sheet membranes developed at GKSS is the envelope type module. [174] A membrane envelope consists of two membrane sheets which are thermally welded at their edge. Between the membrane sheets, spacer material is located, facilitating the permeate withdrawal in radial direction towards a centrally located permeate tube. Membrane envelopes and permeate tube form a membrane stack which is subdivided into compartments by means of baffle plates and mounted inside a pressure vessel. The purpose of the subdivision is to keep the feed flow velocity close to constant and hence minimize the formation of boundary layers. Especially concentration boundary layer formation should be controlled, since these boundary layers cause concentration polarisation. This phenomenon leads to an increase of concentrations of the retained components towards the membrane surface whilst the concentrations of the preferentially permeating components are decreasing. The result is a decrease in separation driving force for those components to be separated from the feed stream whilst an increased driving force is exerted on the components to be retained. Depending on the application, the subdivision can be asymmetric in order to account for the reduction of feed flowrate by permeation. Other design features are the short permeate path, a packing density up to 500 m 2 /m 3 , a relatively low number of gaskets and the thermal welding of the membrane envelopes, i.e. no glue is required for joining the membrane leafs. The module concept has been applied to micro-, ultraand nanofiltration, reverse osmosis, pervaporation, vapour and gas permeation. Hence different versions of the module exist for liquid and gas phase separations and for feed side pressure levels up to 150 bar. The superior operating performance of this module design has been thoroughly investigated, for example in. [175-179]

For most applications, the module can be represented by the assumption of unhindered permeate withdrawal (see Fig. 24). texts.orig: The most prominent area of application of hollow fibre membrane modules is dialysis for the treatment of patients with kidney diseases. [1] At GKSS this concept was developed further to result in the fibre-in-fibre reactor. This special module represents a bio-artificial kidney. Hollow fibres with smaller diameter (e.g. PAN-NVP) and larger (e.g. PAN) were prepared so that the smaller fitted into the larger, with a distance of 50-100 l m, filled with kidney epithelial cells in between. This system provided the best cell growth conditions, indicated by a tight attachment of cells on the membrane surface and subsequent proliferation and development of structural elements of normal ephitelia. [184] Asimilar development is the duomodule. It integrates two kinds of serially connected hollow fibre filters into one module housing. The inner compartments of both filters are interlocked by a stable connection into which a throttle valve is integrated. The outer compartments are filled with an incompressible fluid. The combination of filtration and re-filtration between the compartments in such a filter system demonstrates a high fractionation ability. [185,186]

texts.orig: However, the principal advantage of the hollow fibre membrane module type, i.e. its high packing density of up to 10 000 m 2 /m 3 , led to its widespread use for other applications. One example is the successful application of these modules to recover hydrogen from large scale process streams in refineries, methanol or ammonia plants. [3,37,187] Hollow fibre modules are available for liquid and gas phase applications and are the preferred membrane geometry for membrane contactors. Next to flat sheet membranes, GKSS has developed hollow fibre membranes, too. They consist of porous PEI and have internal diameters of 500 to 700 l m. The internal fibre surface can be coated by a dense separation layer consisting of PDMS or a cellulose based, water vapour selective material. An application that has been licensed by industry is the drying of compressed air. For this purpose a low cost module was designed, where part of the dried retentate stream is throttled by an integrated valve. This portion of dried gas is employed as a purge gas on the permeate side, flowing counter-currently to the retentate, thus decreasing the permeate water partial pressure and hence increasing the driving force.

texts.orig: Membrane contactors have a separation principle different to conventional membrane processes. Here the membrane does not act as the means of separation but provides a well defined mass transfer area that separates the two phases of an absorption process. Typically, the absorbent is supplied to the lumen side of the hollow fibres whereas the gas phase is flowing on the shell side of the contactor. Within the scope of an EU project, [188] the application of such a contactor to humidity control of air was investigated. In order to prevent contamination of the air by the lithium-chloride solution used as the absorbent, the hollow fibres were coated with a thin, dense PDMS layer. The module is equipped with circular fibre frames containing hollow membranes arranged in parallel. Adjacent frames can be rotated by an angle of 15° to achieve an effective mass transfer. [189] The transversal flow pattern and the module design are illustrated in Figure 26.

texts.orig: The described membrane modules are typically part of increasingly complex processes and hence have to operate in combination with other unit operations as compressors and vacuum pumps to provide the driving force, heat exchangers and other separation units. Frequently, recycle loops, phase changes, multicomponent mass transfer effects and non-ideal thermodynamic behaviour have to be considered. In order to account for these effects and devise the optimum process, commercial process simulation software is employed. These software packages contain thermodynamic property packages, rigorous numerical routines, graphical flowsheeting environments and libraries of models for standard unit operations. Membrane processes as a relatively new technology are not part of these libraries and hence it is necessary to develop simulation models which depict the operating performance of membrane modules compatible to the commercial process simulators. A vast amount of publications deals with the development of models for membrane modules. Some examples are: [187,190-194] However, these contributions treat membrane units on a stand-alone basis. Models compatible to commercial process simulators are published more seldom. [177,195-198,201] Research at GKSS was concentrated on the development of models for the description of the envelope type module for gas- and vapour permeation applications. texts.label: text
texts.orig: From 1982 to 1992 GKSS has operated a deep diving facility as research tool to develop new welding techniques for off-shore plants. Manned diving tests have been performed up to 600 m depth. The divers breathing gas was a so-called trimix, a mixture of helium, oxygen and nitrogen. The oxygen content was controlled according to the diving depth. The trimix was contaminated because of generation of CO2 by breathing and argon. Argon was used as shield gas for welding operations. The contaminated gas had to be reclaimed for reuse. The standard operation for helium reclamation is cryogenic condensation. Because of the narrow ice point of oxygen and argon, the helium reclaimer did not perform as expected and an alternative process for helium purification was required.

texts.orig: The development of a membrane based helium reclamation unit was the first realized gas separation project at GKSS in 1985. A new integral asymmetric gas separation membrane based on polyetherimide was developed. [203] The helium/nitrogen selectivity of the membrane was in the range of > 200 based on single gas measurements. [9] The existing membrane module for water desalination by reverse osmosis was modified according to the requirement of gas separation applications. The polyetherimide membrane was cast as a flat sheet membrane which was then converted to so-called membrane envelopes. The membrane stack was divided into compartments in order to control the flow velocity over the membrane surface in dependence of the reduction of feed volume flow because of helium permeation through the membranes. texts.orig: The driving force for the development of membranes and membrane processes for organic vapour recovery was the amendment of the German 'Technical Instructions on Air Quality Control - TI Air'. Existing off-gas purification systems had to be modified and improved in order to meet the new stringent air quality requirements. In spring 1987 GKSS was contacted by an industrial consortium regarding the possibility to separate gasoline vapours by means of membranes from off-gas generated by handling and loading of gasoline. This was the initiator to develop thin film composite membranes with a selective layer based on silicon rubber. The newly developed organic vapour selective membrane consists of a nonwoven backing material made of polyester, a porous polyetherimide substrate and a thin selective layer of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). The lab scale membrane casting technique had to be converted into a pilot scale production because no industrial company was available to produce the new membrane. A membrane module has been designed to treat the expected volume flows and process design tools have been developed to calculate the separation process based on data obtained from lab scale and pilot scale tests. [174] The operating principle of this module type is shown in Figure 25.

texts.orig: There are several potential applications of membrane technology in natural gas conditioning (Fig. 32). GKSS has concentrated on hydrocarbon and water vapour dewpointing. The hydrocarbon dewpointing membrane was tested and improved in the frame work of the Brite- EuRam III project 'Dehydration and hydrocarbon dewpointing of natural gas by membrane technology'. [212] Closely related was a second project dealing with the treatment of associated gas from oil production. In cooperation with a consortium of 3 natural gas producers membrane performance to control the methane number of gas generated by oil / water separation was evaluated. The gas generated by oil production was used as fuel gas for a gas engine. The fluctuating content of higher hydrocarbons has a severe impact on the anti knocking behaviour of the fuel gas. It was demonstrated that the methane number of associated gas could be enhanced by means of membrane technology from 35 to an average > 50 in dependence of the operating conditions. [213] A methane number of > 50 is required in order to provide a reliable operation performance of a gas engine. The membranes for natural gas treatment have to be designed according to the expected higher operating pressure at high organic vapour concentrations. The superstructure of the composite membrane had to be improved in order to avoid membrane compaction. The microporous substrate made of PEI was replaced by PAN. [214] PAN with an improved pore structure is now the basis for most of the commonly used organic vapour selective composite membranes. Based on the pilot project experience first commercial units have been commissioned by GKSS licensees.

texts.orig: The second approach was the development of a membrane suitable for water vapour dewpointing. Most of the polymers for gas and vapour separation have a high permeance for water vapour. The real challenge was the development of membranes suitable for the use in natural gas applications. The water vapour content in natural gas has to be reduced to approx. 50 ppm in dependence of regulations, pressure and ambient conditions in order to avoid condensation and hydrate formation in pipes and valves. The target is the required dewpoint reduction at minimum of product losses. This requires a membrane with a water vapour/methane selectivity of 1000 to 10 000. To use the full potential of the provided selectivity, a pressure ratio according to the membrane selectivity has to be applied. [212] The other option to apply the required driving force is the use of sweep gas at the permeate side. [215] The use of sweep gas in the counter current mode is only possible with hollow fibre modules. Water vapour dewpointing of natural gas is at the threshold to become a commercial product.

texts.orig: The hydrocarbon vapour recovery membranes are chemically stable in a gaseous organic environment. It is self-evident to test the membrane material also for liquid separations. The elastomeric selective layer of the composite membrane swells in presence of organic compounds. The swelling causes higher permeation rates. Tests have shown that the degree of swelling depends on the degree of crosslinking of the elastomeric material. The rubbery membranes have been tested for organophilic nanofiltration. Only the highly crosslinked membranes have shown an acceptable molecular cut-off in dependence of the organic compound. [220]

The elastomeric selective layer of the composite membrane swells in presence of organic compounds. The swelling causes higher permeation rates. Tests have shown that the degree of swelling depends on the degree of crosslinking of the elastomeric material. The rubbery membranes have been tested for organophilic nanofiltration. Only the highly crosslinked membranes have shown an acceptable molecular cut-off in dependence of the organic compound. [220]

texts.orig: The hollow fibre membrane development started at the Geesthacht branch in 1988. The first developments were all based on PEI. Following the comprehensive research approach suitable modules (Fig. 33) were developed, too. The first technical application was the use of hollow fibre modules to introduce hydrogen into water. The company Solvay developed the so-called KNR ® -process to convert nitrate in potable water into nitrogen by means of a heterogeneous catalyst. [228, 229] The water has been aerated with hydrogen before the catalytic conversion of nitrate happens. Hydrogen was lost because of the formation of bubbles and the incomplete solubility of the bubbles. The use of a hollow fibre device facilitated a bubble-free introduction of hydrogen and the reduction of hydrogen demand. [230]

texts.text: Pervaporation is a technology to separate minor compounds from a liquid phase by means of dense membranes. In dependence on the separation task hydrophilic or organo-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: philic membranes are used. A liquid feed mixture is in contact with the membrane. To generate a driving force the partial vapour pressure of the compounds is reduced by applying a vacuum to the permeate side. Permeate leaves the membrane in the vapour phase and is condensed. The condensation is carried out under vacuum in order to keep the capacity of the required vacuum pump small. Heat is necessary to evaporate the permeating components. Due to the consumed heat of evaporation the feed stream is cooled down. Since the trans-membrane flux typically depends on the temperature according to an Arrhenius type relationship, the temperature decrease causes a decrease in performance. In order to control this performance decrease, pervaporation units typically consist of a variety of membrane modules and heat exchangers connected in series. The latter are employed for reheating the feed mixture. [3] Pervaporation can be considered as a competing technology to distillation. Pervaporation requires only the heat of evaporation to separate the minor compounds from a feed mixture, whereas distillation requires the evaporation of the complete feed mixture. The drawbacks of pervaporation are the complex operation and the high investment costs. Hence distillation will probably remain the preferred separation technology for systems with high relative volatilities for some time yet. Membrane units have their applications for systems with low relative volatilities or those that exhibit azeotropic points, i.e. no further separation by means of distillation is possible. Thus it appears logical to combine the advantages of the two unit operations to form a hybrid process. [3,177] Distillation can be used to concentrate a mixture up to the azeotropic point whilst membranes are employed to split the azeotropic mixture. Shown in Figure 34 is a vapour permeation unit instead of a pervaporation unit. Hence the reheating of the feed can be avoided. Employing vapour permeation in combination with distillation is especially advantageous, since feed is already available as a vapour.

texts.orig: The first approach to pervaporation was the separation of high boiling compounds from aqueous solutions. The membrane polymer used was a segmented elastomeric polyetheramide block copolymer. In order to stabilize the membrane structure a woven metal wire mesh was incorporated into the pervaporation membrane. Electric power could be supplied to the metal wire mesh to heat the membrane in order to avoid a temperature decrease of the feed liquid. [233] A second application was the separation of vanillin from a binary aqueous solution from a fermentation broth. [234] Based on these experiences other separation experiments of flavours from fermentation broth have been performed. [235]

texts.orig: Very hard pressing problems of mankind are water and energy resources. Although membranes play already some role today in water desalination or in fuel cell technology, the presently available membranes still suffer from quite a few shortcomings. The high permeability of methanol in membranes presently used for the DMFC, for example, require better materials, where proton conduction is improved and methanol permeability stays small.

texts.orig: For the time being, the goal for future development is to increase permeability of dialysis membranes in medicine up to the cut-off point of the molecular weight of albumine. Future work will have to focus on combined removal strategies i.e. via filtration and adsorption in blood purification. [237] For sure, in regenerative medicine the bioartificial organs as hybrid systems with membranes as separation materials or scaffolds will play an important role. Membrane-controlled proliferation of cells on membrane surfaces will become another important field of the applications of membranes. They will require well-designed membranes both in terms of their chemical structure, morphology and surface topography.

texts.orig: Gas separation is also an aspect of chemical technology where energy intensive unit operation as cryogenic condensation and distillation are often employed. Gas permeation processes have been applied to some of the separation problems (see Section 3.4) as the removal of VOCs from permanent gas streams. However, numerous other separation tasks could be tackled once membranes with an improved selectivity towards higher hydrocarbons, carbon dioxide or hydrogen become available in conjunction with membrane modules designed for high throughput operation and appropriate process design and simulation tools.
